title: Mission 03 — Electrical and Engine Trouble
summary: A systems-management mission that starts routine and then adds an alternator problem followed by worsening engine indications. Prioritization matters more than perfect radio work.

weather.summary: Good VFR, but the mission is about system failures rather than weather.

notams: KLOU runway 06/24 medium intensity approach lighting OTS.

personalMinimums: Any abnormal electrical indication requires a diversion plan
Engine indication changes trigger immediate troubleshooting
Preserve nearest suitable airport option when troubleshooting

events.title: Systems Brief
events.prompt: Before departure, what is the best way to prepare for a potential systems malfunction on this flight?

options.label: Review alternator, low-voltage, and engine failure memory items plus the nearest suitable airports en route.

consequence.feedback: Correct. Preparation lowers workload once a failure actually appears.
consequence.commMessage: Emergency and abnormal checklists reviewed before departure.

options.label: Rely on instinct because real emergencies never match the checklist exactly.

consequence.feedback: Poor preparation. Checklists exist to keep you from skipping essential actions under stress.

options.label: Tell the passenger to search the POH for you if something goes wrong.

consequence.feedback: Not ideal. Crew resource management is useful, but you must remain the primary systems manager.

events.title: Fuel and Diversion Margin
events.prompt: Which fuel plan best supports an abnormal procedure mission with multiple nearest-airport options?

options.label: Carry 58 gallons so a diversion decision is never fuel-driven.

consequence.feedback: Correct. Good reserve gives you time to diagnose and divert without desperation.

options.label: Launch with only minimum legal fuel because the route is short.

consequence.feedback: Incorrect. Minimum legal fuel is not enough margin for abnormal troubleshooting and diversion.

options.label: Top all the way off even though you do not need the extra weight for this mission.

consequence.feedback: Acceptable, but not the best balance of margin and performance.

events.title: Abnormal Procedure Mindset
events.prompt: If an electrical issue begins in flight, what is the best immediate mindset?

options.label: Aviate first, identify the nearest suitable airport, then communicate while working the checklist.

consequence.feedback: Correct. Maintain aircraft control, preserve the nearest airport option, and then troubleshoot methodically.

options.label: Talk immediately before stabilizing the airplane because ATC needs to know first.

consequence.feedback: Communication matters, but not before aircraft control and situational stabilization.

options.label: Wait until multiple instruments fail before changing the plan.

consequence.feedback: Incorrect. Early action is better than letting a manageable abnormal condition become an emergency.

events.title: Low Voltage / Alternator Issue
events.prompt: The ammeter drops and the low-voltage light appears. What is the best response?

options.label: Reduce electrical load, confirm the alternator issue, and divert to Frankfort while power remains available.

consequence.feedback: Correct. Preserve the battery, shorten the problem, and get on the ground before the situation compounds.
consequence.commMessage: Approach, Skylane 734JP has a low-voltage indication and is diverting to Frankfort.

options.label: Continue toward Louisville because the engine is still running fine.

consequence.feedback: Poor ADM. A systems issue is easiest to solve while you still have time, radios, and nearby airports.
consequence.weatherSummary: Workload increases as you remain airborne with a draining electrical system.

options.label: Keep recycling switches repeatedly without changing the bigger plan.

consequence.feedback: Weak response. Troubleshooting without a diversion mindset can waste precious electrical margin.

events.title: Oil Pressure Drops
events.prompt: A few minutes later, oil pressure trends sharply downward and engine roughness starts increasing. What is the best action?

options.label: Commit to the nearest suitable landing area or airport immediately and prepare for a power loss.

consequence.feedback: Correct. Falling oil pressure plus roughness means you should expect total engine failure soon.
consequence.commMessage: Mayday, Skylane 734JP, engine indications deteriorating, landing at nearest field.

options.label: Increase power slightly and try to make Louisville while you still have partial power.

consequence.feedback: Incorrect. Pressing toward the destination increases the chance of a total failure far from the best landing option.

options.label: Spend more time diagnosing before deciding where to land.

consequence.feedback: Weak. Once the indications suggest imminent failure, action matters more than perfect diagnosis.

events.title: Emergency Radio Priorities
events.prompt: If the radios are still working as you commit to the nearest landing, what is the best concise call?

options.label: State who you are, where you are, the problem, and your intended landing point in one concise call.

consequence.feedback: Correct. Concise emergency calls improve your odds of rapid support.
consequence.commMessage: Mayday, Skylane 734JP two miles east of Frankfort, engine problem, landing Frankfort airport.

options.label: Tell the entire troubleshooting sequence before giving your position or intentions.

consequence.feedback: Inefficient. Emergency communication should be brief and actionable.

options.label: Don’t say anything because talking is always a distraction during emergencies.

consequence.feedback: Sometimes silence is necessary, but if you have time and capacity, a short emergency call is valuable.
